cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the history experts' statements can prove about the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A through D. First, I need to recall the relevant part of the passage. The passage mentions that two sisters wrote "Good Morning to All" in 1893, and people started using it as a birthday song. Then it says that history experts say there's no record of who wrote the actual "Happy Birthday" lyrics, and that the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. So the history experts are pointing out that the song's origins are unclear in terms of who wrote the birthday lyrics and that it was used by the public before any company claimed it. The question is about what this proves. The options are: A. Does not belong to Warner Music Group B. Has more than 200 years' history C. Was initially owned by another company D. Has always been very popular Looking at B: The song was written in 1893, so 2023 minus 1893 is 130 years. So it's less than 200, so B is incorrect. C: The passage says Warner has claimed it since 1988, but the experts aren't talking about another company owning it first. The original song was by the Hill sisters, but the lyrics aren't attributed to anyone. So C doesn't seem right. D: The passage says it became popular with the public long before being owned by a company, but "has always been very popular" is a bit tricky. The experts don't mention popularity in their statement directly, just that it was being sung before being published. But the question is what their statements can prove. So maybe not D.
